batchNote: This folder's name ('Sea trials') does not describe its actual contents -- all 9 files are Code of Federal Regulations reference volumes, not sea-trial reports or test logs. Flagged here rather than silently corrected, since the mismatch itself may be worth asking Vinci about (was this the intended folder?). Per the assigned task, this intake covers extraction only -- no draft questions were created and course.json was not touched.

Not extracted further -- would only matter if an instructor wants the underlying construction-standard citation.

items.cfrReference: 33 CFR Parts 1-124, Subchapters A-K (includes Part 84, International & Inland Navigation Rules, whose Annex IV is 'Distress Signals')
items.extractionStatus: partial -- table of contents captured, target section not reached
items.notes: This is a 722-page volume; the table of contents explicitly lists 'Annex IV: Distress Signals' as a section, which is the official U.S. list of internationally recognized distress signals (pyrotechnic signals, SOS, MAYDAY, EPIRB, SART, flag/shape signals, etc.) -- exactly the kind of source that could verify or refine chapter-5-signaling content already live (e.g. the red-star-signal fact in the already-promoted q-quiz1a-redstar-signal). The plain-text extraction of this volume was cut off (tool-side truncation on a large file) before reaching Part 84's body text, so the Annex IV content itself was not captured or verified in this pass.

items.cfrReference: 46 CFR Parts 159-165, Subchapter Q -- Equipment, Construction, and Materials: Specifications and Approval
items.extractionStatus: extracted (partial -- Subparts 159.001 through 160.028 in full; later subparts TOC-only, see needsFollowUp)
items.extractionConfidence: high for the extracted range, low for the un-reached later subparts
items.extractedTitle: 46 CFR Subchapter Q, Part 159 (Approval of Equipment and Materials) and Part 160 (Lifesaving Equipment), Subparts 160.001 through 160.028
items.extractedText:
Subpart 160.001 (Life Preservers, General), §160.001-2: 'A life preserver must be of such construction, material and workmanship that it can perform its intended function in all weathers...' (b) 'A life preserver must be capable of supporting a minimum of 22 pounds in fresh water for 48 hours.' (c) 'Life preservers which depend upon loose or granulated material for buoyancy are prohibited.' (e)/(f) a life preserver must support the wearer face-up and turn an unconscious/exhausted wearer to a safe flotation position with the face held clear of the water. (h) must be highly visible: Indian Orange, International Orange, or Scarlet Munsell Red.

Subpart 160.010 (Buoyant Apparatus for Merchant Vessels): defines buoyant apparatus as flotation equipment OTHER than lifeboats, liferafts, and personal flotation devices -- box-float type and peripheral-body type, including an inflatable buoyant-apparatus variant that must meet Subpart 160.151 (Inflatable Liferafts SOLAS) with listed exceptions.

Subpart 160.013 (Hatchets, Lifeboat and Liferaft, for Merchant Vessels): hatchets must meet Federal Specification GGG-A-926 (Type I, Class I, Design D or E), be of one approved size/drawing, have a rounded handle hole for a 6-foot lanyard (no sheath permitted), and be permanently marked with the manufacturer's identification. This subpart is a construction/approval spec -- it establishes that a Coast-Guard-approved hatchet is real, required lifeboat/liferaft equipment, but does not itself state how many are carried or where they're placed on the boat (see Document(7).PDF.pdf for that).

Table of contents also lists (not reached in body text): Subpart 160.042 Skids, Liferaft; 160.044 Pumps, Bilge, Lifeboat; 160.051 Inflatable Liferafts for Domestic Service; 160.054 First-Aid Kits for Inflatable Liferafts; 160.055 Life Preservers, Unicellular Plastic Foam; 160.132 Launching Appliances -- Davits; 160.133 Release Mechanisms for Lifeboats and Rescue Boats (SOLAS); 160.135 Lifeboats (SOLAS); 160.151 Inflatable Liferafts (SOLAS); 160.170 Davit-Launched Liferaft Automatic Release Hooks (SOLAS); 160.171 Immersion Suits.
keyFacts: A Type I life preserver must support a minimum of 22 pounds of buoyancy in fresh water for 48 hours (46 CFR 160.001-2(b)).
A Type I life preserver must be designed to turn an unconscious or exhausted wearer's face clear of the water (46 CFR 160.001-2(e)/(f)).
Loose or granulated buoyant material is prohibited in a life preserver (46 CFR 160.001-2(c)).
A Coast-Guard-approved hatchet for lifeboat/liferaft use must meet Federal Specification GGG-A-926 and Coast Guard drawing 160.013-1(b), with a lanyard attached and no sheath (46 CFR 160.013).
examAngles: Which statement about Type I life preservers is true/false? (direct source for the two facts already tested in the already-promoted q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false)
What is the minimum buoyancy required for a Type I PFD? (22 lbs -- not yet a standalone live question, potential future draft, but out of scope for this intake-only task)
items.verifiesOrContradictsLive: VERIFIES two facts underlying q-quiz1a-typeI-pfd-false (already promoted, live on main as of the last merge): the 22-lb minimum buoyancy figure and the face-clear-of-water design requirement both match 46 CFR 160.001-2 exactly. No contradictions found.

items.cfrReference: 46 CFR Parts 166-169, Subchapter R -- Nautical Schools, specifically Part 169 -- Sailing School Vessels
items.extractionStatus: extracted (partial -- Subparts 169.100 through 169.600 in full, including the complete lifeboat equipment table; Subpart 169.800 and Subchapters S-W not reached, see needsFollowUp)
items.extractionConfidence: high for the extracted range, low/not-reached for Subpart 169.800 (Operations) and Part 199 (Subchapter W)
items.extractedTitle: 46 CFR Part 169 -- Sailing School Vessels, Subpart 169.500 (Lifesaving and Firefighting Equipment), Table 169.527 and §169.529 (Description of lifeboat equipment)
items.extractedText:
§169.527/Table 169.527 (Required equipment for lifeboats) itemizes exact quantities by 'Exposed and partially protected waters' vs. 'Protected waters' columns, including: Hatchet -- 2 / 1; Fire extinguisher (motor-propelled lifeboats only) -- 2 / 2; Heaving line -- 2 / None; Life preservers -- 2 / 2; Sea anchor -- 1 / None; Boathooks -- 2 / 1; Oars (units) -- 1 / 1; Provisions (per person) -- 2 / None; Water (quarts per person) -- 3 / None; and 22 other itemized categories.

§169.529(h) Fire extinguishers: 'Each fire extinguisher must be an approved Type B-C, Size I. One must be attached to each end of the lifeboat.'

§169.529(k) Hatchets: 'Hatchets must be approved under subpart 160.013 of this chapter. They must be attached to the lifeboat by individual lanyards and be readily available for use, one at each end of the lifeboat.'

§169.529(l) Heaving line: 'The heaving line must be of adequate strength, 10 fathoms in length, and 1 inch in circumference. It must remain buoyant after being submerged for 24 hours.' (10 fathoms = 60 feet -- this is the LIFEBOAT heaving line, a distinct item/length from the already-promoted LIFERAFT heaving line fact of 100 feet; see verifiesOrContradictsLive.)

§169.529(v) Oars: 'A unit, consisting of a complement of rowing oars and steering oar, must be provided for each lifeboat... except that motor-propelled and hand-propelled lifeboats need only be equipped with four rowing oars and one steering oar.'

§169.207: 'A Certificate of Inspection is valid for 5 years.'
keyFacts: A lifeboat must carry one hatchet at each end (bow and stern) -- 46 CFR 169.529(k).
A motor-propelled lifeboat must carry one fire extinguisher (approved Type B-C, Size I) attached to each end -- 46 CFR 169.529(h).
A lifeboat's heaving line must be 10 fathoms (60 feet) long, distinct from the liferaft's separately-established 100-foot heaving line.
Motor-propelled and hand-propelled lifeboats need only 4 rowing oars + 1 steering oar, rather than the full complement otherwise listed.
A Sailing School Vessel Certificate of Inspection is valid for 5 years.
examAngles: What lifeboat equipment item is required at both the bow and stern? (direct verification source for the held-back q-quiz1a-bow-stern-hatchet)
How long is a lifeboat's heaving line, and how does that differ from a liferaft's heaving line? (potential future compare-and-contrast question -- flagged, not drafted, in this intake-only pass)
items.verifiesOrContradictsLive: VERIFIES the held-back q-quiz1a-bow-stern-hatchet claim exactly: 46 CFR 169.529(k) states a hatchet must be 'readily available for use, one at each end of the lifeboat,' matching that question's marked answer word-for-word in substance. Also verifies the fire-extinguisher-at-each-end fact used as a distractor in that same question. No contradictions found. Separately, flags a potential future confusion point (not a contradiction): the lifeboat heaving line (10 fathoms / 60 ft, this document) and the liferaft heaving line (100 ft, already-promoted q-quiz1a-heaving-line-length) are two different items with two different lengths -- worth keeping distinct in any future drafting, the same way the enclosed-lifeboat-max-speed vs. Class-1-motor-lifeboat-min-speed '6 knots' pair was flagged in the Quiz 1a promotion review.
